transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Caleb, I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’m not really sure. I mean, there’s always a lot going on, but I don’t want to take up too much time talking about myself. I’ve been thinking about how I compare to others at work, I guess. It’s just... sometimes it feels like I’m not doing enough, you know? But maybe it’s not that important to dwell on.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling some pressure about your performance at work and how you measure up to your colleagues. It’s understandable to feel that way, especially in a competitive environment. Can you tell me more about what specifically makes you feel like you’re not doing enough?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I see my colleagues working longer hours and getting their papers published, and I just... I can’t help but think that I’m falling behind. It’s like, I start to worry that I’m not as good as they are. I know I shouldn’t compare myself, but it just happens. I guess I just feel anxious about it sometimes. But, I mean, maybe it’s not a big deal. I don’t want to make it seem like it’s a huge issue or anything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re experiencing a lot of anxiety when you compare yourself to your colleagues, especially regarding their work hours and accomplishments. It’s common to feel that way, but it seems like it’s weighing on you more than you’d like. You mentioned that you know you shouldn’t compare yourself, yet it still happens. What do you think drives that comparison for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I don’t know, maybe it’s just... I’ve always felt like I need to prove myself, you know? Like if I’m not working as hard or producing as much, then I’m not really... valuable, I guess. It’s just this nagging thought that I should be doing more. I try to push it aside, but it keeps coming back. I don’t want to feel this way, but sometimes it’s hard to shake off. I just... I don’t know if I’m ready to dive deeper into it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like there’s a deep-rooted belief for you about needing to prove your value through your work and productivity. That can be a heavy burden to carry, especially when it feels like those thoughts keep resurfacing. It’s completely okay to take your time with this; we can explore it at your own pace. What do you think would help you feel a bit more at ease with these feelings when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess... maybe if I could find a way to remind myself that it’s okay to not always be the best or the busiest. But it’s hard to change that mindset. I sometimes try to distract myself or push through the feelings, but it doesn’t really help in the long run. I just end up feeling more overwhelmed. I’m not sure what would really work for me. I guess I’m still figuring that out.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re recognizing that the strategies you’ve been using, like distraction or pushing through, aren’t really serving you in the way you’d like. It’s understandable to want to find a healthier way to navigate those feelings of overwhelm and pressure. It’s a process to shift your mindset, and it’s okay to still be figuring it out. What do you think it would look like for you to start exploring that acceptance of not always needing to be the best or the busiest?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I think it might involve... just being kinder to myself, maybe? Like, acknowledging that it’s okay to have limits and that everyone has their own pace. But it’s tough to actually put that into practice. I mean, I can say it, but believing it is another story. I guess I worry that if I let go of that pressure, I might not push myself enough. It’s a bit of a catch-22, you know? I’m not sure how to find that balance.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re really reflecting on the importance of self-compassion and recognizing your limits, which is a significant step. I can see how it feels like a catch-22—wanting to be kinder to yourself while also fearing that it might lead to complacency. That tension between self-acceptance and motivation can be really challenging. What do you think might help you start to believe in that kindness toward yourself, even just a little?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I guess... maybe if I could start small? Like, allowing myself to acknowledge when I’ve done something well, even if it’s not as big as what others are doing. It’s just hard to break that habit of focusing on what I think I’m lacking. I could try to remind myself that everyone has their own struggles, even if they don’t show it. But, I don’t know, it feels like a lot to change all at once. I’m just not sure where to start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Starting small sounds like a really thoughtful approach. Acknowledging your own accomplishments, no matter how small, can be a powerful way to shift your focus. It’s understandable that breaking the habit of focusing on what you feel you lack can feel overwhelming. It might help to think about one specific, manageable step you could take to begin this process. What’s one small thing you could do this week to recognize something you’ve done well?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, maybe I could keep a little journal or something? Just to jot down one thing each day that I feel good about, even if it’s minor. I think writing it down might help me see it more clearly instead of just letting it slip away. But, I don’t know, I might forget or feel silly doing it. It’s just... I guess it’s a start, right? I’ll have to see if I can actually stick with it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: A journal sounds like a great idea! Writing down those positive moments can help reinforce that acknowledgment and make it feel more tangible. It’s completely normal to feel a bit silly or unsure about it at first, but it’s also a valuable step toward shifting your mindset. Remember, it’s okay if you miss a day or two; what matters is that you’re trying. How do you feel about giving this a try, even if it’s just for a short time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could give it a shot. It feels a bit daunting, but I like the idea of having something to look back on. I guess it might help me see that I’m doing okay, even if it doesn’t feel like it sometimes. I’ll try to keep it simple and not put too much pressure on myself about it. I just hope I can stick with it. Thanks for suggesting that. It feels like a small step, but maybe it could lead to something more.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re feeling hopeful about the journal idea, and I think keeping it simple is a wise approach. It’s great that you’re allowing yourself the space to explore this without too much pressure. Remember, it’s all about progress, not perfection. I’m glad you found it helpful to talk through this. As you start this journey, how do you think you might celebrate those small wins, even if they feel minor?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess celebrating small wins could be... just taking a moment to appreciate them? Like, maybe treating myself to something I enjoy, or even just taking a break to relax. I think acknowledging those moments, even in a small way, might help reinforce that it’s okay to feel good about what I’ve done. But I’m not really used to celebrating myself, so it might feel a bit strange at first. I’ll have to see how it goes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a lovely way to celebrate those small wins! Taking a moment to appreciate your accomplishments, whether through a treat or a break, can really help reinforce that positive mindset. It’s completely normal for it to feel a bit strange at first, especially if it’s not something you’re used to. Just remember to be gentle with yourself as you explore this new practice. How are you feeling about the steps you’re planning to take?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I’m feeling a bit more hopeful about it, actually. It’s nice to have something to focus on that feels manageable. I still have some doubts, like whether I’ll really stick with it or if it’ll make a difference, but I guess that’s part of the process. I’m just trying to remind myself that it’s okay to take small steps. I appreciate you helping me think through this. It feels a little less overwhelming now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and that our conversation has helped make things feel a bit less overwhelming. It’s completely natural to have doubts, but recognizing that small steps are part of the process is a significant insight. You’re taking meaningful action by being open to this exploration. If you ever want to share how it’s going or discuss anything else, I’m here to support you. What do you think your next step will be?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think my next step will be to actually start that journal, maybe tonight or tomorrow. I’ll try to write down something positive from the day, even if it’s just a small thing. I want to see how it feels to put those thoughts down. I guess I’ll just take it one day at a time and see where it leads me. Thanks for being supportive about this. It helps to have someone to talk to about it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful plan! Starting your journal tonight or tomorrow is a great way to begin this journey. Taking it one day at a time is a wise approach, and I’m glad you feel supported in this process. Remember, it’s all about discovering what works for you. If you ever want to share your experiences or explore anything else, I’m here for you. Wishing you the best as you start this new practice!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. I’ll keep you updated on how it goes, and I’ll try to be patient with myself as I figure it out. It feels good to have a little bit of support. I guess I’ll just take it one step at a time. Thanks for the encouragement!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Caleb! I’m glad you’re feeling supported, and I appreciate your openness in sharing your thoughts. Taking it one step at a time is a great mindset, and I look forward to hearing how it goes for you. Remember to be kind to yourself along the way. If you ever want to talk again or need more encouragement, I’m here for you. Take care, and good luck with your journaling!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks so much! I’ll definitely keep that in mind. I appreciate your understanding and support. It means a lot. I’ll take care and hopefully have some positive things to share next time we talk. Take care, too!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Caleb! I’m glad to have been here to support you. I look forward to hearing about your positive experiences next time we talk. Take care of yourself, and remember to celebrate those small wins along the way. Until next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I’ll do my best to remember that. I appreciate all your support and guidance. Until next time, take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome, Caleb! I’m glad to have been able to support you. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation. Wishing you all the best as you start this new journey!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I really appreciate it. I’ll take care and look forward to our next chat. Wishing you all the best as well!
